questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a current-carrying circular loop is replaced by an equivalent magnetic dipole, a point located on the axis of the loop corresponds to the
questions.solution:
The magnetic dipole moment of a current-carrying circular loop is directed along its axis.

For a magnetic dipole, a point lying on its axis is referred to as being in the end-on position (or axial position).

Therefore, a point located on the axis of the loop corresponds to the end-on position.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a current in a circular loop is equivalently substituted by a magnetic dipole,
options: the distance $d$ separating the poles is constant
the pole strength $m$ for each pole is constant
questions.solution:
The magnetic moment of a circular loop carrying current $I$ and having area $A$ is given by $M = IA$.

When this loop is equivalently substituted by a magnetic dipole with pole strength $m$ and separation distance $d$, its magnetic moment is given by $M = md$.

Equating the two expressions for magnetic moment, we get $md = IA$.

For a given current loop, the current $I$ and area $A$ are constant. Therefore, the product $md$ must be constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let $r$ denote the distance from the centre of a magnetic dipole to a point on its axis. The magnetic field at this point is proportional to
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at a distance $r$ on the axis of a short magnetic dipole of magnetic moment $M$ is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{r^3}$

Therefore, the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the cube of the distance from the centre of the dipole.

$B \propto \dfrac{1}{r^3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, two short magnets with equal dipole moments $M$ are joined perpendicularly at their centres. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at a distance $d$ from the centre along the bisector of the right angle?
questions.solution:
Let the centre of the two magnets be at the origin $(0,0)$.

The horizontal magnet has its South pole on the left and North pole on the right. Its magnetic dipole moment is directed from South to North, so $\vec{M}_1 = M \hat{i}$.

The vertical magnet has its South pole at the bottom and North pole at the top. Its magnetic dipole moment is also directed from South to North, so $\vec{M}_2 = M \hat{j}$.

The net magnetic dipole moment of the system is the vector sum of the individual dipole moments:
$\vec{M}_{net} = \vec{M}_1 + \vec{M}_2 = M \hat{i} + M \hat{j}$

The magnitude of the net dipole moment is:
$|\vec{M}_{net}| = \sqrt{M^2 + M^2} = \sqrt{2}M$

The direction of $\vec{M}_{net}$ is at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the positive x-axis, which is along the bisector of the angle between the two North poles.

The point $P$ is at a distance $d$ along the bisector of the angle between the two South poles. This direction is exactly opposite to the direction of the net dipole moment. Therefore, the point $P$ lies on the axial line of the equivalent net dipole.

The magnitude of the magnetic field on the axial line of a short dipole at a distance $d$ is given by:
$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2|\vec{M}_{net}|}{d^3}$

Substituting $|\vec{M}_{net}| = \sqrt{2}M$, we get:
$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}M}{d^3}$

questions.subject: Physics
options: a line along the north-south direction
questions.solution:
The magnetic meridian at any place is defined as the vertical plane passing through the magnetic axis of a freely suspended magnetic needle at rest under the influence of the earth's magnetic field.

Therefore, the magnetic meridian corresponds to a vertical plane.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a compass needle, which is constrained to rotate in a horizontal plane, is transported to a geomagnetic pole, it
options: will point only in the north-south direction
will become rigid and exhibit no movement
will remain stationary in any arbitrary orientation
will point only in the east-west direction
questions.solution:
At the geomagnetic poles, the Earth's magnetic field is entirely vertical.

The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field, $B_{H}$, is zero at the magnetic poles.

A compass needle constrained to rotate in a horizontal plane aligns itself with the horizontal component of the magnetic field.

Since $B_{H} = 0$, there is no horizontal magnetic force or torque to align the needle in any specific direction.

Therefore, the compass needle will remain stationary in any arbitrary orientation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A dip circle is positioned at the geomagnetic equator. Its needle is permitted to rotate in a vertical plane perpendicular to the magnetic meridian. The needle will remain
options: in any direction except horizontal and vertical
in whatever direction it is released
questions.solution:
At the geomagnetic equator, the angle of dip is zero, which means the vertical component of the Earth's magnetic field is zero ($B_V = 0$).

The horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field ($B_H$) acts along the magnetic meridian.

The dip circle is placed such that its needle rotates in a vertical plane perpendicular to the magnetic meridian. The angle between this plane and the magnetic meridian is $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$.

The horizontal component of the magnetic field in this plane is $B_H' = B_H \cos 90^{\circ} = 0$.

The vertical component in this plane is $B_V' = B_V = 0$.

Since both the horizontal and vertical components of the magnetic field in the plane of rotation are zero, the effective magnetic field in this plane is zero.

Consequently, the magnetic needle experiences no restoring torque about its axis of rotation. Being balanced at its center of gravity, it will remain in neutral equilibrium and stay in whatever direction it is released.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Of the four graphs provided, which one best represents the current-deflection relation for a tangent galvanometer?
questions.solution:
The relation between the current $i$ and the angle of deflection $\theta$ in a tangent galvanometer is given by:

$i = K \tan \theta$

where $K$ is the reduction factor of the galvanometer. Rearranging the formula for $\theta$, we get:

$\theta = \tan^{-1} \left( \dfrac{i}{K} \right)$

Analyzing the function $\theta(i)$:
1. At $i = 0$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0) = 0$. The graph passes through the origin.
2. For small values of $i$, $\theta \approx \dfrac{i}{K}$, so the graph initially has a constant positive slope.
3. As $i \to \infty$, $\theta \to \dfrac{\pi}{2}$. The graph has a horizontal asymptote at $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
4. The slope is $\dfrac{d\theta}{di} = \dfrac{K}{K^2 + i^2}$, which decreases as $i$ increases. This means the curve is concave downward.

Observing the given graphs, curve 'c' starts from the origin, is concave downward, and asymptotically approaches the value $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ on the vertical axis.

Thus, graph 'c' best represents the current-deflection relation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A tangent galvanometer is directly connected to an ideal battery. If the coil's number of turns is doubled, the deflection will
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the center of the coil of a tangent galvanometer is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2r}$.

The current through the coil is $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$, where $V$ is the voltage of the ideal battery and $R$ is the resistance of the coil.

The resistance of the coil is proportional to the total length of the wire, which is proportional to the number of turns $N$ (assuming the radius $r$ of the coil is constant). Let $R = c N$, where $c$ is a constant.

Substituting $I$ in the expression for $B$, we get:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N}{2r} \left( \dfrac{V}{c N} \right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 V}{2 r c}$

This shows that the magnetic field $B$ is independent of the number of turns $N$.

The deflection $\theta$ in a tangent galvanometer is given by $B = B_H \tan \theta$, where $B_H$ is the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field.

Since $B$ remains unchanged, the deflection $\theta$ will also remain unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose a current-carrying circular loop is substituted with its equivalent magnetic dipole. A point situated on the loop lies in the
questions.solution:
A current-carrying circular loop behaves as a magnetic dipole with its magnetic moment vector perpendicular to the plane of the loop.

The equivalent magnetic dipole is considered to be placed at the center of the loop, with its axis aligned along the normal to the plane of the loop.

The broadside-on position (equatorial position) of a magnetic dipole is the plane perpendicular to its axis and passing through its center. This plane coincides exactly with the plane of the circular loop.

Therefore, any point situated on the loop lies in the broadside-on position of the equivalent magnetic dipole.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the current is doubled, the deflection also doubles in
questions.solution:
For a moving-coil galvanometer, the deflecting torque is $\tau = NIAB$ and the restoring torque is $\tau_r = C\theta$.

At equilibrium, $NIAB = C\theta \Rightarrow \theta \propto I$.

Thus, when the current is doubled, the deflection also doubles.

For a hot-wire ammeter and a moving-iron ammeter, the deflection $\theta \propto I^2$.

For a tangent galvanometer, the current $I \propto \tan \theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $a$ carries an electric current $i$. A very long bar magnet is positioned such that its north pole is located at the centre of the loop. The magnetic field produced by the magnet at any point on the periphery of the wire is $B$. The force acting on the wire is
options: very nearly $2\pi a i B$ perpendicular to the plane of the wire
$\pi a i B$ along the magnet
$2\pi a i B$ in the plane of the wire
questions.solution:
The north pole of a very long bar magnet acts approximately as a magnetic monopole. When placed at the centre of the circular loop, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by it at any point on the periphery of the wire is directed radially outward.

Let the current element of the circular loop be $d\vec{l}$. Since the wire is circular and the field is radial, the angle between the current element $d\vec{l}$ (which is tangential) and the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is $90^{\circ}$.

The magnetic force on the small current element is given by:
$d\vec{F} = i(d\vec{l} \times \vec{B})$

The magnitude of this force is:
$dF = i \cdot dl \cdot B \sin 90^{\circ} = i B dl$

By the properties of the cross product, the direction of $d\vec{F}$ is perpendicular to both $d\vec{l}$ and $\vec{B}$, which means it is perpendicular to the plane of the circular wire.

Integrating this force over the entire circumference of the loop, the total force is:
$F = \int i B dl = i B \int dl = i B (2\pi a) = 2\pi a i B$

Since a real bar magnet is not an ideal point monopole (the pole is distributed and slightly inside the magnet), the magnetic field may have a very small component non-parallel to the plane. Hence, the net force is very nearly $2\pi a i B$ and is directed perpendicular to the plane of the wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose $r$ represents the distance of a point on the axis of a bar magnet from its centre. The magnetic field at this location is proportional to
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a bar magnet of length $2l$ and magnetic dipole moment $M$ at a point on its axial line at a distance $r$ from the centre is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2Mr}{(r^2 - l^2)^2}$

Therefore, the magnetic field is proportional to $\dfrac{r}{(r^2 - l^2)^2}$.
